content.text: What it is

Agentic vs assistive triage is a structured decision process for determining the appropriate level of AI autonomy for a specific use case. It classifies each use case as fully agentic (AI acts end-to-end without human approval per transaction), human-in-loop agentic (AI acts but a human can review and reverse before consequences are finalized), constrained agentic (AI acts autonomously only above a confidence threshold, escalating low-confidence cases to humans), or assistive (AI provides recommendations but humans make every decision).

The triage outcome is not just a technical design decision—it determines governance requirements, regulatory compliance posture, monitoring obligations, and the organizational change management burden. Getting the classification wrong in either direction—over-automating a high-risk process or under-automating a low-risk one—has significant operational and compliance consequences.
Why it matters

The term 'AI use case' encompasses a wide spectrum of autonomy levels, from a copilot that drafts emails for human review to an agent that autonomously places financial holds, routes documents, and triggers downstream workflows with no per-transaction human involvement. Treating all use cases as equivalent in governance, monitoring, and delivery design is both over-engineered for simple assistive cases and dangerously under-governed for fully agentic ones.

Triage at the discovery stage creates the right design contract before delivery begins. It prevents the common pattern where a use case is designed as assistive (for speed), deployed in production, and gradually configured to operate autonomously without explicit governance review—the 'autonomy creep' pattern that creates significant regulatory and operational risk.
Benefits
Creates an explicit, documented autonomy contract for each use case that constrains scope creep and autonomy drift during delivery
Determines governance requirements upfront: human-in-loop agentic needs different monitoring than fully assistive
Identifies regulatory constraints early: some processes are legally required to have human decision-makers regardless of AI capability
Enables appropriate risk controls: agentic use cases need circuit breakers, rollback mechanisms, and automated anomaly detection; assistive cases need recommendation quality metrics
Provides the architecture team with the autonomy profile needed to design appropriate oversight and intervention mechanisms
Supports portfolio risk management: the ESC can see the distribution of autonomy levels across the portfolio and ensure it reflects organizational risk appetite
Trade-offs and limitations
Triage requires understanding regulatory and policy constraints that may not be fully known at discovery time
Process reversibility is sometimes ambiguous—a decision may be formally reversible but practically irreversible due to customer communication that cannot be unsent
Constrained agentic (confidence-threshold gating) requires knowing what confidence level is achievable before the model is trained
Autonomy levels set at triage may need revision after production deployment reveals actual error rates and reversibility patterns
Over-conservative triage (defaulting to assistive) reduces AI value realization; over-aggressive triage creates compliance exposure
How it works

Triage uses a four-question decision tree. Q1 asks whether every action in the process can be executed without case-by-case human approval. If no, the use case is classified as Assistive—the human decision authority cannot be delegated to AI regardless of capability, either because organizational policy prohibits it or because the process inherently requires human judgment. If yes, proceed to Q2.

Q2 asks whether the consequence of a wrong AI action is fully reversible within one business day without customer harm. If no—if an error triggers an irreversible customer action (contract signed, funds transferred, public disclosure made)—the use case requires human-in-loop agentic design: the AI acts but a human must confirm or reverse within a defined review window before consequences are finalized. If yes, proceed to Q3.

Q3 asks whether process inputs are structured and auditable enough for the AI to interpret reliably without ambiguous context. If no, the use case is classified as constrained agentic: the AI handles high-confidence inputs autonomously and escalates ambiguous cases to humans. Q4 asks whether regulation or policy explicitly requires a human decision-maker. If yes, the use case is Assistive regardless of technical capability.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Triage decision tree showing four questions that classify use cases as Assistive, Human-in-Loop Agentic, Constrained Agentic, or fully Agentic

content.text: The tree is designed so that every no answer produces an immediate classification—teams do not need to traverse the full tree for most use cases. In practice, the majority of enterprise use cases at initial triage land in Assistive or Constrained Agentic, with fully Agentic classification reserved for high-volume, structured, low-consequence processes.
When to use it
During use-case discovery, immediately after opportunity backlog scoring assigns a Tier 1 or Tier 2 classification
When a use case's autonomy requirements are unclear and the architecture team needs a documented design contract before sprint planning
When a deployed assistive use case is being proposed for autonomy upgrade—re-triage with updated answers based on observed performance
When a regulatory change or new policy is introduced that may change the Q4 answer for existing use cases
When an incident occurs in production and the team needs to re-evaluate whether the autonomy level was appropriate
How to implement
Run the four-question triage for every use case that passes opportunity backlog scoring review, as part of the design intake process

Involve the business process owner (who answers Q1 and Q2 from operational knowledge), the compliance lead (who answers Q4 from regulatory knowledge), and the technical architect (who assesses Q3 from model capability knowledge)
Document the triage outcome, the answer to each question, and the rationale in the program's use-case registry
Set the autonomy level as a constraint on the technical architecture: the delivery team designs to the triage outcome, not to maximum possible autonomy
Include the autonomy distribution across the portfolio in the ESC quarterly report—the ESC should be aware of how many agentic versus assistive use cases are in production
Re-triage any use case before autonomy is increased in production, even if the change seems minor
Examples

# Triage assessment questionnaire

TRIAGE_QUESTIONS = [
    {
        'id': 'Q1',
        'question': 'Can every action in this process be executed without '
                    'case-by-case human approval?',
        'no_implies': 'Assistive — human retains decision authority',
        'yes_next': 'Q2',
    },
    {
        'id': 'Q2',
        'question': 'Is the consequence of a wrong AI action fully reversible '
                    'within one business day without customer harm?',
        'no_implies': 'Human-in-the-loop agentic — AI acts, human reviews '
                      'before consequences are finalized',
        'yes_next': 'Q3',
    },
    {
        'id': 'Q3',
        'question': 'Does the process have structured, auditable inputs that '
                    'the AI can reliably interpret without ambiguous context?',
        'no_implies': 'Assistive or constrained agentic with confidence '
                      'threshold gating',
        'yes_next': 'Q4',
    },
    {
        'id': 'Q4',
        'question': 'Does existing regulation or policy permit automated '
                    'decisions for this process without mandatory human sign-off?',
        'no_implies': 'Assistive — regulatory constraint forces human decision',
        'yes_next': 'AGENTIC_CANDIDATE',
    },
]

# Triage output table — portfolio example

Use Case                   | Q1  | Q2  | Q3  | Q4  | Outcome
---------------------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|------------------
Invoice 3-way match        | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Agentic
Claims routing (low-value) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Agentic
Claims decision (complex)  | No  | -   | -   | -   | Assistive
KYC document verification  | Yes | Yes | Yes | No  | Assistive (reg.)
Email intent classification| Yes | Yes | No  | Yes | Constrained Agentic
Loan approval              | No  | -   | -   | -   | Assistive (policy)
Appointment scheduling     | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Agentic
Fraud hold placement       | Yes | No  | Yes | Yes | Human-in-Loop

Notes:
  '-' = question not reached due to earlier No answer
  Constrained Agentic: AI handles inputs above 0.85 confidence;
                        escalates below threshold to human

# Autonomy level implementation mapping

AUTONOMY_IMPLEMENTATION = {
    'Agentic': {
        'pattern': 'Fully autonomous loop',
        'human_role': 'Exception review (SLA-based, not per-transaction)',
        'monitoring': 'Automated output sampling + drift detection',
        'example': 'Invoice matching: AI reads invoice, matches to PO, '
                   'posts journal entry, files document — no human touch',
    },
    'Human-in-Loop Agentic': {
        'pattern': 'AI acts, human reviews within SLA',
        'human_role': 'Review AI action before it is finalized; '
                      'can reverse within review window',
        'monitoring': 'Override rate, review SLA compliance',
        'example': 'Fraud hold: AI places 48h hold; fraud analyst reviews '
                   'and confirms or releases within 24h',
    },
    'Constrained Agentic': {
        'pattern': 'AI acts only above confidence threshold',
        'human_role': 'Handles all cases below threshold; '
                      'reviews escalated cases',
        'monitoring': 'Threshold hit rate, escalation rate, accuracy split '
                      'above vs below threshold',
        'example': 'Email routing: AI routes if confidence > 0.85; '
                   'routes to human queue if below',
    },
    'Assistive': {
        'pattern': 'AI presents recommendation; human decides',
        'human_role': 'Owns every decision; AI is an information tool',
        'monitoring': 'Recommendation acceptance rate, '
                      'decision quality vs AI suggestion',
        'example': 'Loan approval: AI generates risk summary and '
                   'recommendation; underwriter makes final decision',
    },
}
Real-world example

A financial services firm triaging its AI portfolio found that invoice three-way matching and appointment scheduling both passed all four triage questions and were classified as fully agentic. Fraud hold placement passed Q1 and Q3 but failed Q2: once a fraud hold is placed and the customer is notified by SMS, the customer experience impact is immediate and reverting within 24 hours does not undo the notification. This made fraud hold placement Human-in-Loop Agentic—the AI places the hold flag but a fraud analyst must confirm within four hours before the automated customer SMS is sent.

KYC document verification failed Q4: EU AML regulations require a named human officer to sign off on customer identity verification for high-risk customers. Despite the AI being technically capable of making the determination, the regulatory framework mandated Assistive classification for this segment. The firm avoided a compliance issue that would have arisen had triage not been conducted before deployment.
Common mistakes
Defaulting to assistive without running the triage questions, missing automation opportunities on low-risk, high-volume processes
Treating 'technically reversible' as equivalent to 'reversible in practice'—a contract signed by a customer may be legally reversible but practically permanent
Not involving compliance in Q4—technical teams often assume automation is permitted when specific regulatory prohibitions exist
Allowing autonomy creep: a use case classified as assistive during discovery gradually accumulates automated actions without a formal re-triage
Treating triage as a one-time event rather than a trigger-based review; process changes, new regulations, and model capability improvements all warrant re-triage
Using a single autonomy classification for a use case that has mixed-autonomy sub-processes—complex workflows may have agentic steps and assistive steps that need independent classification
Best practices
Document the triage outcome as a formal constraint in the use case specification, signed by the business owner and compliance lead
For Q2 edge cases (formally reversible but practically difficult), default to human-in-loop agentic and revisit at 90 days of production data
Set the confidence threshold for constrained agentic use cases before delivery, not after—require the model to demonstrate it can hit the threshold in holdout evaluation before production
Include re-triage triggers in the production monitoring specification: if error rate exceeds X percent or regulatory guidance changes, re-triage is required
Report autonomy level distribution to the ESC quarterly; a portfolio skewed heavily toward agentic warrants board-level risk awareness
Test the reversibility mechanism for human-in-loop agentic cases in staging before production; reversal paths often have bugs that are only discovered under real-world conditions
Performance and scalability

For large portfolios with 50 or more use cases, triage sessions are a throughput constraint. Batch similar use cases and run group triage sessions where a process category (e.g., all document processing use cases) is triaged together. The Q4 regulatory question often has consistent answers across a process category, enabling pre-completion of that dimension before the individual session.

Triage decisions accumulate into an organizational policy library over time. Capture recurring triage patterns as guidance: 'all payment-initiation use cases default to human-in-loop agentic under current regulation' or 'all read-only data access use cases pass Q1–Q3 and require only Q4 assessment.' This reduces session time and improves consistency.
Tooling and ecosystem
Triage decision documentation: Confluence, SharePoint, or a custom use-case registry with autonomy classification fields
Regulatory reference: regulatory compliance platforms (Archer, ServiceNow GRC) that map regulations to process categories
Architecture design: standard architecture patterns for each autonomy level, maintained in the AI CoE playbook
Monitoring requirements by autonomy level: predefined dashboards and alerting thresholds in the MLOps platform
Autonomy portfolio tracking: a Power BI dashboard showing the distribution of triage outcomes across the AI portfolio by business unit

Security and reliability

Agentic use cases pose a different risk profile than assistive ones: errors propagate without human review gates, and adversarial inputs can trigger harmful actions at scale without the friction of human approval. For all agentic and human-in-loop agentic use cases, the security review must include adversarial input testing—prompt injection for LLM-based agents, boundary testing for decision models, and circuit breaker testing to verify that the automated rollback mechanism actually activates.

The triage document itself is a governance artifact that records which processes have been granted autonomous AI execution authority. Treat it as a controlled document: changes to triage outcomes for production use cases must go through the ESC or the relevant working group, not through informal team decisions. Unauthorized autonomy upgrades in production are a governance breach.
Key takeaways
Agentic vs assistive triage classifies each use case into one of four autonomy levels—Agentic, Human-in-Loop Agentic, Constrained Agentic, or Assistive—using four structured questions
The classification determines governance requirements, monitoring design, regulatory compliance posture, and architecture patterns; it is not just a label
Q4 (regulatory constraint) is the most commonly underassessed question; involve compliance before assuming autonomous operation is legally permissible
Autonomy creep—gradual expansion of AI autonomy without re-triage—is the highest-risk gap in enterprise AI governance and must be actively prevented
The triage outcome is a production constraint that requires ESC or governance working group approval to change, not a technical team decision
